cards.front: The basic unit of a chemical element, consisting of a nucleus surrounded by electrons.
A group of two or more atoms held together by chemical bonds; the smallest particle of a compound.
An atom or molecule that has gained or lost one or more electrons, giving it a net electrical charge.
The three main particles that make up an atom.
The number of protons in the nucleus of an atom; it uniquely identifies an element.
The total number of protons and neutrons in an atom's nucleus.
Atoms of the same element that have different numbers of neutrons.
A positively charged ion (formed when an atom loses electrons).
A negatively charged ion (formed when an atom gains electrons).
The state of matter with a definite shape and volume, where particles are closely packed in a fixed arrangement.
The state of matter with a definite volume but no definite shape; particles are close but can slide past each other.
The state of matter with no definite shape or volume; particles are far apart and move freely.
The process by which a solid changes directly into a gas without passing through the liquid phase (e.g., dry ice).
The reverse process where a gas changes directly into a solid (e.g., frost formation).
A change in the form or state of matter, but not its chemical identity (e.g., melting ice, boiling water, dissolving sugar).
A process that changes the chemical identity of a substance, forming new substances with different properties (e.g., rusting iron, burning wood).
A pure substance consisting entirely of one type of atom, which cannot be broken down by chemical means.
A substance made of two or more different elements that are chemically bonded together in a fixed ratio.
A combination of two or more pure substances that are physically combined and can be separated by physical means.
A mixture where the components are not uniformly distributed and individual components are visible (e.g., salad, sand and iron filings).
A mixture with a uniform composition throughout; also called a solution (e.g., salt water, air, vinegar).
The principle that matter is neither created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction; total mass remains constant.
A chemical bond formed when two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons, typically between nonmetals.
A chemical bond formed by the complete transfer of electrons from one atom (usually a metal) to another (usually a nonmetal), resulting in oppositely charged ions.
The technique used to separate an insoluble solid from a liquid by passing it through a porous material (e.g., filter paper).
The technique used to separate a dissolved solid from a liquid by heating to remove the liquid as vapor, leaving the solid behind.
The technique used to separate two or more liquids with different boiling points by heating and condensing the vapors.
The technique used to separate mixtures based on the different speeds at which components travel through a stationary phase (paper) using a mobile phase (solvent).
The technique used to separate magnetic materials (like iron) from non-magnetic materials in a mixture.
The technique used to separate two immiscible (non-mixing) liquids, such as oil and water, using a cone-shaped glass vessel.
The technique used to separate a mixture where one component sublimes (turns directly from solid to gas) upon heating, leaving the other component behind.
The technique used to separate a solid from a liquid by gently pouring the liquid off the top after the solid has settled.
The technique used to separate a dissolved solid from a liquid by crystallizing the solid out of a saturated solution.
The technique used to separate a solid from a liquid by spinning the mixture at high speed, causing the denser solid to settle at the bottom.
The most important piece of safety equipment that must be worn at all times in the lab to protect against chemical splashes.
The first action to take if a chemical splashes into your eyes.
cards.back: Use the eyewash station for 15-20 minutes

cards.front: The type of footwear required in a chemistry lab to protect feet from spills and broken glass.
The safe technique for smelling a chemical in the lab by fanning vapors toward your nose.
The immediate response to getting a chemical on your skin.
cards.back: Rinse with copious water for 15 minutes

cards.front: The direction a test tube must point when being heated to prevent spraying hot contents on anyone.
The acronym for the proper use of a fire extinguisher (Pull, Aim, Squeeze, Sweep).
The final, essential step to complete before leaving the laboratory to remove any chemical residue.
